exercises.title: Why video is 10-100× harder than images
exercises.prompt: A 10-second 1080p clip is ~1.5 GB of raw pixels. Explain the three core challenges video generation adds over images: spatiotemporal compression, temporal coherence, and compute budget.
exercises.hint: A video is a 3D tensor (T×H×W). Need a VAE that compresses across time too; frames must stay consistent (identity/lighting/motion); training is 10-100x image cost.
exercises.solution:
A video is a **3-D tensor** (frames × height × width) where an image is 2-D — the theory carries over but the scale and the new time axis create three challenges:

1. **Spatiotemporal compression.** A 10-s 1080p@24fps clip = 240 frames × 1920×1080×3 ≈ **1.5 GB raw** — pixel-space diffusion is hopeless. You need a **video VAE that compresses across *time* as well as space**, encoding the clip into **spatiotemporal patches/latents** (downsample temporally too, not just per-frame). Compressing redundancy *between* frames is what makes the sequence short enough to model.

2. **Temporal coherence.** Frames can't be generated independently — they must **share content, lighting, and object identity over seconds**, and exhibit **plausible, smooth motion**. The model has to **model motion and consistency**: a character's face, a car's color, the scene's lighting must persist; objects must move continuously (no flicker, no morphing, no popping in/out). This is the hardest part and the main quality differentiator between video models.

3. **Compute budget.** Because each sample is a whole sequence, **training is 10-100× more expensive** than images for the same model size — more tokens/patches per sample, longer attention (now over space *and* time), and far more data movement. This is why frontier video (Sora, Veo, Kling) came later than images and why open-weights video (CogVideoX, HunyuanVideo, WAN) trails closed models by ~12 months: it's gated by compute, not just ideas.

The substrate is the same as Stable Diffusion (VAE + latent diffusion + DiT), just lifted to 3-D — but the time axis turns every cost and every consistency requirement up by an order of magnitude.

exercises.title: Spacetime patches and the DiT video recipe
exercises.prompt: Sora-style video models tokenize video into spacetime patches and run a Diffusion Transformer over them. Explain how spacetime patching works, why treating video as a sequence of patches (like ViT/tokens) is powerful, and how attention handles the temporal dimension. What does this unify with the rest of the course?
exercises.hint: Video VAE → latent volume → cut into 3D (space+time) patches → flatten to a token sequence → DiT denoises. Patches enable variable resolution/duration. Attention (full or factorized spatial+temporal) ties frames together. Unifies with ViT/transformer 'tokenize anything'.
exercises.solution:
**Spacetime patching:** first a **video VAE encodes the clip into a latent volume** (compressed in time and space). That volume is then **cut into 3-D patches** spanning a small region of space **and** a few frames of time, and each patch is **linearly projected into a token embedding** — exactly ViT's 'image → patches → tokens', extended with a temporal extent. The video becomes a **1-D sequence of spacetime tokens**, which a **Diffusion Transformer (DiT)** denoises (predicting noise on the token sequence), conditioned on text via cross-attention + CFG, then the VAE decodes back to frames.

**Why patches are powerful:**
- **One unified representation** for the whole clip — the model sees space and time as just 'more tokens', so the same transformer machinery applies.
- **Variable resolution and duration:** because it's a token sequence, you can train/generate at **different resolutions, aspect ratios, and clip lengths** by simply using more or fewer patches (Sora's flexibility) — no fixed architecture per format.
- **Scalability:** token sequences ride transformer **scaling laws**, so more compute/params reliably improves quality.

**How attention handles time:** self-attention over the spacetime tokens lets **any patch attend to patches in other frames**, which is precisely how the model enforces **temporal coherence** (an object in frame 1 'sees' itself in frame 50). Because **full 3-D attention is O(N²)** over a huge token count, models often **factorize** it — alternating **spatial attention** (within a frame) and **temporal attention** (across frames at the same location), or windowed/strided patterns (Lesson 7.15) — to make the quadratic cost tractable while still propagating information across time.

**What it unifies:** this is the course's recurring 'tokenize anything, feed a transformer' theme (Phase 7.09 ViT, 7.10 Whisper). Images = 2-D patches, audio = spectrogram frames, **video = spacetime patches** — all become token sequences for the **same transformer/DiT substrate**. Video generation isn't a new paradigm; it's **latent diffusion + ViT-style tokenization + DiT**, lifted to 3-D, which is why progress in image diffusion transfers almost directly to video.

exercises.title: Temporal coherence, long-video generation, and world models
exercises.prompt: Temporal coherence over long clips is the central unsolved-ish problem. Explain the failure modes (drift, flicker, identity loss, physics violations), the techniques used (3D/factorized attention, latent temporal compression, autoregressive/sliding-window extension, conditioning on previous frames), the quality-vs-length-vs-compute tradeoffs, and why video models are increasingly framed as 'world models' (and the limits of that claim).
exercises.hint: Long videos drift because attention can't span all frames and errors accumulate; extend via chunked/autoregressive generation conditioned on prior frames (risk: error accumulation). World-model framing: learns physics/object permanence implicitly; limits: no explicit physics, hallucinates, no guaranteed consistency/controllability.
exercises.solution:
**Failure modes of long video:**
- **Drift:** content slowly changes over time (a face morphs, colors shift, style wanders) because the model can't keep the whole clip globally consistent.
- **Flicker:** high-frequency inconsistency between adjacent frames (textures/lighting jitter).
- **Identity loss:** objects/characters lose their identity or count (a dog becomes a different dog, an extra limb appears).
- **Physics violations:** objects pass through each other, water flows wrong, things teleport, gravity/momentum look off — the model has no explicit physics, only learned correlations.
These worsen with **length** because (a) attention can't tractably span *all* frames of a long clip, and (b) any **autoregressive extension accumulates errors**.

**Techniques:**
- **3-D / factorized spatial+temporal attention** to propagate consistency across frames at acceptable cost; windowed/strided attention for long sequences.
- **Latent temporal compression** (video VAE) so more seconds fit in the modeled token budget.
- **Autoregressive / sliding-window extension:** generate the clip in **chunks**, each **conditioned on the last frames of the previous chunk** (and/or a global summary), to extend beyond what one forward pass can hold. Risk: **error accumulation/drift** across chunks.
- **Conditioning on previous frames / keyframes** (image-to-video, frame interpolation, keyframe-then-fill) to anchor consistency.
- **Better VAEs and more compute**, plus motion/optical-flow-aware losses and consistency regularizers.

**The tradeoffs:** it's a **three-way tension between length, quality, and compute**. Full attention over a long clip gives the best coherence but is **O(N²)**-prohibitive; chunked/autoregressive generation enables **arbitrary length** but trades **global coherence** (drift) for tractability; higher resolution/fps multiplies cost again. You pick: short + coherent + high-res, or long + cheaper + drift-prone — you can't yet have all three. This is exactly why most production models cap clips at a few seconds and stitch.

**Why 'world models' — and the limits:** to generate coherent video, a model must *implicitly* learn a lot about the world: **object permanence** (things persist when occluded), **3-D structure and camera motion**, **material/lighting behavior**, and **rough physics/causality** — because predicting plausible future frames *requires* an internal model of how the world evolves. Hence the framing of Sora/Veo and successors as **'world simulators'**, and the interest in them for **robotics/planning** (predict consequences of actions) and as a path toward general physical understanding.
**Limits of the claim:** these models have **no explicit physics engine, no guaranteed consistency, and no symbolic state** — they learn **statistical correlations**, so they **hallucinate**, violate conservation laws, and fail on novel/long-horizon dynamics. Their 'understanding' is **implicit and unreliable**, not a verifiable simulation; controllability (making *exactly* the motion you want) is weak, and they can't guarantee an object behaves consistently. So 'world model' is a useful *aspiration and partial reality* — they clearly encode substantial world knowledge — but in 2026 it's an **emergent, approximate** competence, not a true simulator, and the gap (physical correctness, long-horizon coherence, controllability) is precisely the open research frontier of video generation.
